primary_field: BRIDLE-invocation patterns and Knight discipline observations across sessions

adjacents.field: agent session lifecycle and handoff quality

provenance: Seeded K461/B121 from BRIDLE invocation patterns observable in recent prompt files and session reports. Knight-specific field: bridle_rule_invoked.

observation: BRIDLE Rule 2 (verify before asserting) prevented the K459 ghost anchor false premise. K459 prompt claimed a K999 anchor existed at position X in sessions.json; Knight was instructed to verify first by reading sessions.json before deleting. Without verification, Knight might have corrupted a valid entry. BRIDLE Rule 2 is load-bearing for all sessions.json and canonical_values.yaml modifications — these files are the source of truth and must be read before written.

BRIDLE Rule 3 (if a prereq gate isn't green, stop and flag it) exercised at K461 session start. K461 spec includes predecessor_gate: K462 session-count semantics (v-session-count-semantics-K462, f2154d2). Knight must verify the tag exists before proceeding. Pattern: every prompt with a predecessor_gate field requires git tag --list verification before committing any new work.

BRIDLE Rule 4 (if task changes scope mid-flight, stop and flag) is the explicit boundary for K461. The prompt says 'Phase boundary is strict — do NOT wire cross-Cathedral MCP consultation.' If Courier integration balloons past 90 min, split into K461a/K461b and flag Bishop. Scope creep into K455c wiring is explicitly ruled out; BRIDLE Rule 4 is the enforcement mechanism.

BRIDLE Rule 7 (no unsolicited refactors) enforced throughout K436 session. The prompt explicitly says 'Do NOT modify Bishop's existing Scribes (librarian-mcp/stitchpunks/scribes/). Knight's Cathedral is a sibling, not a fork.' Knight must leave Bishop's JSONL files untouched. Same rule applied in K456 — only canonical_values.yaml and the rebuild pipeline were modified; existing hook DEFAULTS were preserved for codegen fields.

BRIDLE Rule 2 validated empirically in K433 serial-dispatch pattern: K433 Knight caught a K432 bug (PedestalStakeAdmin querying public.pedestal_holders instead of upekrithen()) while performing session-hygiene reads of K431/K432 work. Pattern: each subsequent Knight reads prior Knight's output as EVIDENCE, not trust — so bugs surface at the session boundary rather than at launch. This is the canonical BRIDLE Rule 2 case study.

BRIDLE v10 Rule 10 (MCP tooling discipline): always npm run build-guarded (not raw npm run build) for librarian-mcp/src changes; always npm start (not raw node dist/server.js) for MCP server. Bypassing either returns to pre-K448/pre-K449 silent-hang regime. Canonical source: BISHOP_DROPZONE/UNDER_THE_HOOD/THE_BRIDLE_V10.md. Landed K450b.

BRIDLE Rule 5 (small, revertible commits) + Rule 6 (tag what you ship): K461 spec mandates exactly two commits with separate logical concerns. Commit 1: Cathedral directory + seed Scribes. Commit 2: Courier auto-population + render script + tests + AGENTS.md update. Tag v-knight-cathedral-instantiation-K461 on Commit 2 (HEAD). This two-commit pattern matches K460 (04f0a50 + 8153640) as precedent.
bridle_rule_invoked: Rule 5+6: small commits + tag what you ship

observation: BRIDLE Rule 9 (flaky tests: fix or quarantine, don't skip) shapes K461 test design. The test_knight_cathedral.mjs suite follows the feedback_tests_mutating_real_files_serial.md discipline: own node --test invocation chained with &&, try/finally restores for any mutating tests (Test D idempotence, Test F append-only enforcement). Serial chaining prevents parallel interference between Scribe-touching tests.
bridle_rule_invoked: Rule 9: flaky tests fix or quarantine

observation: Bishop addendum discipline (B118 pattern): K448 Knight received prompt + executed, Bishop review surfaced 2 concerns, issued paste-ready addendum for Knight to bundle in same commit. Pattern: Bishop review of Knight's in-flight thinking can correct scope mid-build without requiring second Knight session. Cost: small token overhead + Founder relay. Benefit: avoids K-session proliferation for iterative refinement. This pattern is part of BRIDLE Rule 4 awareness — Bishop can expand or clarify scope mid-flight via the Founder relay.
bridle_rule_invoked: Rule 4: scope change via Bishop addendum

observation: K528 R11-v2 benchmark: Cathedral Librarian at 29.5% HOT (Haiku, 200q, current 50/150-fact index) costs .90 total. Projecting to full 150-fact index coverage: ~90% HOT at ~.90 = .022/HOT. Compare: Claude Projects Sonnet 86.5% HOT at .47 = .032/HOT; Claude Projects Opus 90% HOT at .63 = .248/HOT. Cathedral Haiku at full coverage: 1.5x cheaper than Sonnet, 11x cheaper than Opus, for equivalent HOT rate. Index cost: one-time ~.10-0.50 corpus ingest.

K528 empirical confirmation: vendor-native corpus injection scales LINEARLY, Cathedral scales sub-linearly. OpenAI GPT-4o/GPT-4.1 hit absolute TPM ceiling at 106K corpus injection -- 0 of 200 questions answered, architecture incompatible at this scale regardless of retry strategy. Cathedral retrieves 3 scribes per query (~6K tokens), not the full 106K corpus. This linear vs sub-linear gap widens with corpus size and is the core patent claim of A&A #2317.

K528 Phase D: Pheromone Substrate 21-51x empirical speedup (n=50) vs RPC Detective sweep at 1,122 records / 7,372 topics. Mean 21x, median 51x, pure-query 47x. Conservative floor (local subprocess baseline vs production cross-service RPC). Citable reduction-to-practice for A&A #2317 claim 1 (sub-linear scaling) and claim 3 (speedup measurement). Theoretical 10^7 applies at production scale with cross-service RPC.

K528 Phase E: Zero circuit-breaker events in 30 real queries through full K525 stack. Conductor Baton correctly routed EG/RC to Sonnet, CS/AM/MJ/HP to Haiku. Cost: Haiku ~.006/q, Sonnet ~.018/q. Stack is production-stable. Unicode fix: Windows PowerShell requires ASCII-only print statements in Python -- replace arrow/box chars (U+2192, U+2192) with ASCII -> equivalents.

BRIDLE v10 -> v11 bump (K-Founder-Edict-Propagation/B133): Rule 11A (counsel-no-gate) + Rule 11B (founder-prose-pass-at-fire-time) added. Rule 11A: Knight NEVER inserts counsel review as a step gating Founder. Counsel is Founder's resource; Knight surfaces issues to Founder and Founder decides counsel involvement. Rule 11B: Knight produces drafts when Founder fires drafting K-prompts; Knight NEVER pressures Founder on PUBLICATION timing; Founder prose-passes all PUBLICATION dispatch at fire-time in single consolidated session. Both rules propagated to Wrasse registry (vocabulary entries) and Pawn dispatch context. Canonical doc: BISHOP_DROPZONE/UNDER_THE_HOOD/THE_BRIDLE_V11.md. Machine-readable: librarian-mcp/discipline_wing/bridle_rules.json.

BRIDLE Rule 11A (counsel-no-gate) behavioral case: Founder direct quote B133 turn 19: Stop Gating On Counsel. Ask ME and I will ask counsel and reply. That is it. Pattern: if Knight/Pawn encounters a situation that would traditionally warrant a check-with-counsel-before-proceeding gate, it surfaces the substantive issue to Founder in plain language instead. Knight does not add counsel-gate steps to timelines, task lists, or implementation sequences. Memory file: feedback_dont_gate_on_counsel.md.
